freshness.reason: Dual-source opportunity-roi-v3 run 2026-06-12. Xiaohongshu corpus collected 2026-06-12 (56 posts/50 authors); re-check market+community Aug-Oct 2026.

assumptions: Single focused project: a from-scratch LLM inference engine, benchmarked per stage against production systems (vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp) and then optimized to outperform the baseline in a defended regime. Final deliverable: a paper-like technical report.
MVP-first: ship a working, measurable engine in Weeks 1-2, then add one mechanism at a time and re-benchmark — never build everything before measuring.
Outperformance thesis: not beating vLLM/SGLang on datacenter GPUs at scale, but in a defended regime — Apple Silicon, memory-constrained (16GB), low-to-moderate concurrency.

Depth over breadth: implement the named-paper core (Orca, Sarathi-Serve, PagedAttention) plus ONE differentiating optimization deeply, chosen data-driven from the Week-8 baseline; the rest are optional-extension ablation rows.
Execution window Jun 22 - Sep 13, 2026, ~17 committed + ~3 reserve h/week, ~182h committed with ~22h slack.
The report is written incrementally: each module produces a section (design + that stage's benchmark delta), so Week 12 is assembly and polish.

candidates.label: LLM inference engine & serving-systems engineer
candidates.thesis: Build a from-scratch LLM inference engine MVP-first, benchmark every stage against production systems (vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp) across cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon, then implement one paper-backed optimization — chosen data-driven from the baseline gap — to outperform the production baseline in a defended regime (Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low-to-moderate concurrency). The deliverable is a paper-like technical report with an ablation ladder: each mechanism's measured contribution, plus the regime-specific win.
skill_axes: LLM inference internals: paged KV cache, continuous batching, chunked prefill
Rigorous measurement: per-stage ablation ladder, cross-device benchmark vs vLLM/SGLang/llama.cpp
Data-driven optimization (KV quant/offload, speculative decoding) for a regime win + a paper-like report
same_person_problem_set: Implement PagedAttention, Orca batching, and Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill and prove parity with a reference
Benchmark the engine against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp on the same traces across CUDA and Apple Silicon
Implement a paper-backed optimization and beat the production baseline in a specific, defended regime
candidates.proof_artifact: A from-scratch inference engine (paged KV, continuous batching, chunked prefill, admission), a per-stage cross-device benchmark vs vLLM/SGLang/llama.cpp, one optimization that outperforms the baseline in a defended regime, and a paper-like technical report tying it together with an ablation ladder.
falsification_conditions: Inference-systems roles stop rewarding from-scratch engine work when re-checked Aug-Oct 2026
The engine cannot reach a defensible fraction of vLLM/SGLang throughput on CUDA, or beat llama.cpp on Apple Silicon
No paper-backed optimization yields a reproducible win in any defended regime

candidates.label: On-device / Apple-Silicon inference engineer
candidates.thesis: Same engine, but center the whole story on Apple Silicon: exploit unified memory and Metal to beat llama.cpp on concurrency and long-context in 16GB. Narrower, but a very clean hardware-advantage story.
same_person_problem_set: Beat llama.cpp on the M4 with continuous batching + quantized/offloaded KV
candidates.proof_artifact: An Apple-Silicon-native engine that beats llama.cpp on concurrency and long-context in 16GB.
falsification_conditions: On-device-only reads as too narrow for the broader systems roles

roles.name: ML Systems Engineer
roles.summary: Makes inference/training fast and correct: serving stacks, quantization, performance. The niche home base.

roles.name: AI Infrastructure Engineer
roles.summary: Platforms for the ML lifecycle: serving infra, data/training platforms, reliability.

roles.name: AI Software Engineer
roles.summary: Builds AI-powered products: evals, LLM integration, backend glue.

roles.name: Software Engineer
roles.summary: Generalist builder: services, tooling, reliability. The widest entry door.

roles.name: AI Research Engineer
roles.summary: Turns research into systems. Practitioner evidence: PhD-gated, crowded for new grads.

workflow_stages.name: Serving
workflow_stages.description: Inference runtime: batching, KV cache, latency.

phases.title: From-scratch inference engine · 12 weeks
phases.outcome: An MVP-first from-scratch inference engine, benchmarked per stage against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across devices, with one data-driven optimization that outperforms the production baseline in a defended regime — written up as a paper-like report.
phases.description: MVP engine + harness -> paged KV -> continuous batching (Orca) + admission -> chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + full cross-engine benchmark -> outperformance optimization (3 weeks) -> paper-like report + release.
phases.milestone: A per-stage ablation ladder, a cross-engine/device benchmark, one regime-specific outperformance result, and a paper-like technical report.

sections.title: Weeks 1-2 · MVP engine + benchmark harness (Mac-first)
sections.integration_checkpoint: The streaming API serves the model on the M4; the oracle is frozen; the harness reports MVP-vs-llama.cpp numbers reproducibly; the report's baseline section is written.
sections.name: Weeks 1-2 · MVP engine + benchmark harness (Mac-first)

sections.title: Weeks 3-4 · Paged KV cache (PagedAttention) + re-benchmark
sections.integration_checkpoint: Paged decode matches the oracle; the re-benchmark attributes a reduced-fragmentation / higher-concurrency delta over the MVP; the paging section is written.
sections.name: Weeks 3-4 · Paged KV cache (PagedAttention) + re-benchmark

sections.title: Weeks 5-6 · Continuous batching (Orca) + admission + re-benchmark
sections.integration_checkpoint: Continuous batching matches the oracle per sequence, shows a throughput/goodput gain over static (and the llama.cpp delta), returns 429 with bounded p99 under overload; the section is written.
sections.name: Weeks 5-6 · Continuous batching (Orca) + admission + re-benchmark

sections.title: Weeks 7-8 · Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + full cross-engine benchmark
sections.integration_checkpoint: Chunked prefill matches the oracle; a reproducible matrix reports the engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across CUDA and Apple Silicon; a defended target regime is chosen; the section is written.
sections.name: Weeks 7-8 · Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + full cross-engine benchmark

sections.title: Weeks 9-11 · Outperformance optimization (3 weeks, data-driven)
sections.integration_checkpoint: The optimization preserves quality and produces a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; the optimization section is written; optional extensions are noted.
sections.name: Weeks 9-11 · Outperformance optimization (3 weeks, data-driven)

sections.title: Week 12 · Paper-like technical report + portfolio release
sections.integration_checkpoint: A paper-like report with an ablation ladder, the cross-engine benchmark, and the regime win — every number reproducible from checked-in configs; the repo and README are published.
sections.name: Week 12 · Paper-like technical report + portfolio release

lessons.title: Weeks 1-2 · MVP engine + benchmark harness (Mac-first)
lessons.problem: The MVP: a correct, instrumented single-request engine you fully understand, a deterministic oracle, and a benchmark harness — all Mac-local. No paging, no batching, no cloud yet. This is the always-working baseline every later stage is measured against.
lessons.why_it_matters: MVP-first means you always have a measurable, shippable artifact. The harness and oracle, built now, make every later 'I made it faster' claim attributable and reproducible.
concepts: serving metrics (TTFT, TBT, p99, goodput, memory)
lessons.build_it: Implement a single-request decode loop + OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint (sampling, stop conditions, cancellation).
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the benchmark harness; check parity against the oracle; record the ablation delta.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests and the measurement, and write the corresponding report section.

exercises.prompt: Implement a single-request decode loop + OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint (sampling, stop conditions, cancellation).
exercises.completion_gate: The streaming API serves the model on the M4; the oracle is frozen; the harness reports MVP-vs-llama.cpp numbers reproducibly; the report's baseline section is written.

exercises.prompt: Run the first incremental benchmark: MVP engine vs llama.cpp on the M4; write the report's 'baseline' section.
exercises.completion_gate: Measured, reproducible, parity-checked, and written into the report.

tests_and_measurements: A working MVP engine, a frozen oracle, a Mac benchmark harness, and the first MVP-vs-llama.cpp numbers.
lessons.proof_artifact: A working MVP engine, a frozen oracle, a Mac benchmark harness, and the first MVP-vs-llama.cpp numbers.
lessons.objective: Build a single-request decode loop over a small model with an OpenAI-compatible streaming API, a deterministic token oracle, and a Mac-local benchmark harness (throughput, TTFT, TBT, p99, memory) over seeded traces; record the first benchmark — the MVP vs llama.cpp on the M4.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, the seeded traces, and the running report; production baselines for the benchmark module.
lessons.architecture_config:
engine: Python over PyTorch (MPS); model: Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B
API: OpenAI-compatible /v1/completions streaming; sampling, stop conditions, cancellation
oracle: deterministic seeded path; harness: throughput, TTFT, TBT, p99, peak memory; seeded traces (short, mixed, bursty)
first benchmark: MVP vs llama.cpp on the M4 (the incremental baseline begins here)
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the decode loop, streaming API, oracle, and harness. Delegate matmul/attention to PyTorch. Mac-only here — cloud CUDA and multi-engine comparison arrive in Weeks 7-8 once there is a mature engine worth comparing.
implementation_steps: Implement a single-request decode loop + OpenAI-compatible streaming endpoint (sampling, stop conditions, cancellation).
Freeze a deterministic oracle fixture (prompt -> token IDs) for every later parity check.
Build the Mac-local benchmark harness (throughput, TTFT, TBT, p99, memory) and seeded traces.
Run the first incremental benchmark: MVP engine vs llama.cpp on the M4; write the report's 'baseline' section.
lessons.exit_gate: The streaming API serves the model on the M4; the oracle is frozen; the harness reports MVP-vs-llama.cpp numbers reproducibly; the report's baseline section is written.

lessons.title: Weeks 3-4 · Paged KV cache (PagedAttention) + re-benchmark
lessons.problem: Contiguous KV wastes memory and caps concurrency. PagedAttention's fixed-size blocks + per-request block tables are the biggest memory lever and the foundation of the memory-constrained outperformance story.
lessons.why_it_matters: On 16GB, KV is the ceiling. Paging is what later lets the engine fit more concurrency / longer context than baselines. Re-benchmarking now isolates paging's exact contribution (an ablation row).
lessons.build_it: Implement a fixed-size KV block allocator with a free list and per-request block tables.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the benchmark harness; check parity against the oracle; record the ablation delta.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests and the measurement, and write the corresponding report section.

exercises.prompt: Implement a fixed-size KV block allocator with a free list and per-request block tables.
exercises.completion_gate: Paged decode matches the oracle; the re-benchmark attributes a reduced-fragmentation / higher-concurrency delta over the MVP; the paging section is written.

exercises.prompt: Re-benchmark: paged vs MVP (utilization, max concurrency) + vs llama.cpp; write the report's paging-ablation section.
exercises.completion_gate: Measured, reproducible, parity-checked, and written into the report.

tests_and_measurements: A PagedAttention KV cache with oracle parity and an attributed memory/concurrency delta over the MVP.
lessons.proof_artifact: A PagedAttention KV cache with oracle parity and an attributed memory/concurrency delta over the MVP.
lessons.objective: Implement a PagedAttention-style KV cache (block allocator, block tables, reference-counted blocks, paged attention); verify oracle parity; then re-benchmark to attribute the memory/concurrency delta over the MVP.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, the seeded traces, and the running report; production baselines for the benchmark module.
lessons.architecture_config:
kv: fixed-size blocks; per-request block table; free list; reference-counted blocks
attention: gather KV from blocks (paged kernel via FlashInfer/torch or a correctness gather)
incremental benchmark: paged vs MVP (utilization, max concurrency at fixed memory) + vs llama.cpp
paper: vLLM / PagedAttention (SOSP'23)
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the allocator, block tables, free list, and paged gather. Delegate matmul/softmax to PyTorch. Parity before performance; benchmark the delta.
implementation_steps: Implement a fixed-size KV block allocator with a free list and per-request block tables.
Implement paged attention; verify token-for-token parity with the oracle.
Add on-demand allocation, free-on-completion, and reference counting for shared blocks.
Re-benchmark: paged vs MVP (utilization, max concurrency) + vs llama.cpp; write the report's paging-ablation section.
lessons.exit_gate: Paged decode matches the oracle; the re-benchmark attributes a reduced-fragmentation / higher-concurrency delta over the MVP; the paging section is written.

primary_sources.title: Efficient Memory Management for LLM Serving with PagedAttention

primary_resource.name: CMU 15-445 Database Systems
primary_resource.section: CMU 15-445 Buffer Pool / page management (block-allocation analog)

lessons.title: Weeks 5-6 · Continuous batching (Orca) + admission + re-benchmark
lessons.problem: Static batching wastes the device and collapses under load. Orca schedules per decode iteration; admission control holds SLOs and sheds excess. By the end of this module the un-optimized system is feature-complete enough to compete.
lessons.why_it_matters: Continuous batching is the biggest throughput lever (10-20x) and what lets the engine beat llama.cpp on concurrency on Apple Silicon. The re-benchmark gives the batching ablation row.
lessons.build_it: Implement an iteration-level scheduler (admit/step/evict each decode iteration).
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the benchmark harness; check parity against the oracle; record the ablation delta.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests and the measurement, and write the corresponding report section.

exercises.prompt: Implement an iteration-level scheduler (admit/step/evict each decode iteration).
exercises.completion_gate: Continuous batching matches the oracle per sequence, shows a throughput/goodput gain over static (and the llama.cpp delta), returns 429 with bounded p99 under overload; the section is written.

exercises.prompt: Re-benchmark: continuous vs static + vs llama.cpp; write the report's batching-ablation section. The un-optimized system is now complete.
exercises.completion_gate: Measured, reproducible, parity-checked, and written into the report.

tests_and_measurements: An Orca continuous-batching scheduler with admission, oracle parity, and an attributed throughput/goodput delta.
lessons.proof_artifact: An Orca continuous-batching scheduler with admission, oracle parity, and an attributed throughput/goodput delta.
lessons.objective: Implement Orca iteration-level scheduling on the paged cache (ragged, padding-free batches) plus a bounded queue with token/KV-aware admission, overload 429s, and cancellation; re-benchmark the throughput/goodput delta.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, the seeded traces, and the running report; production baselines for the benchmark module.
lessons.architecture_config:
scheduler: per-iteration admit/step/evict; ragged batch; per-sequence position ids + masks
admission: bounded queue; token-budget + paged-block-availability; overload -> 429; timeout + cancellation
incremental benchmark: continuous vs static batching + vs llama.cpp (throughput, goodput under load)
paper: Orca (OSDI'22)
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the scheduler, batch membership, padding-free masking, admission arithmetic, and overload behavior on the W3-4 cache. Per-sequence oracle parity before optimizing.
implementation_steps: Implement an iteration-level scheduler (admit/step/evict each decode iteration).
Build ragged, padding-free batches with per-sequence position ids and masks; free KV on completion.
Add a bounded queue with token/KV-aware admission, overload 429s, timeouts, and cancellation.
Re-benchmark: continuous vs static + vs llama.cpp; write the report's batching-ablation section. The un-optimized system is now complete.
lessons.exit_gate: Continuous batching matches the oracle per sequence, shows a throughput/goodput gain over static (and the llama.cpp delta), returns 429 with bounded p99 under overload; the section is written.

primary_sources.title: Orca: A Distributed Serving System for Transformer-Based Generative Models

primary_resource.name: OSTEP
primary_resource.section: OSTEP scheduling + concurrency for an iteration-level inference scheduler

lessons.title: Weeks 7-8 · Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + full cross-engine benchmark
lessons.problem: Finish the scheduling stack with chunked prefill, then — now that the engine is complete-but-unoptimized — run the full apples-to-apples comparison against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon. This baseline tells you which dimension to attack.
lessons.why_it_matters: You cannot pick the right optimization without knowing where you stand. This module produces the honest baseline gap and names the target regime — the data that drives Weeks 9-11.
lessons.build_it: Implement chunked prefill + stall-free batching; verify oracle parity; sweep the Pareto frontier per device.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the benchmark harness; check parity against the oracle; record the ablation delta.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests and the measurement, and write the corresponding report section.

exercises.prompt: Implement chunked prefill + stall-free batching; verify oracle parity; sweep the Pareto frontier per device.
exercises.completion_gate: Chunked prefill matches the oracle; a reproducible matrix reports the engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across CUDA and Apple Silicon; a defended target regime is chosen; the section is written.

exercises.prompt: Name the target regime (where you match/beat: Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low concurrency); write the report's benchmark section.
exercises.completion_gate: Measured, reproducible, parity-checked, and written into the report.

tests_and_measurements: A Sarathi-Serve chunked-prefill scheduler and a reproducible cross-engine, cross-device benchmark that names the target regime.
lessons.proof_artifact: A Sarathi-Serve chunked-prefill scheduler and a reproducible cross-engine, cross-device benchmark that names the target regime.
lessons.objective: Implement Sarathi-Serve chunked prefill + the latency-throughput Pareto, then stand up vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp and run the shared traces through every engine on both devices; report the matrix and choose the target regime.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, the seeded traces, and the running report; production baselines for the benchmark module.
lessons.architecture_config:
prefill: token-budgeted chunks piggybacked with decode (stall-free); Pareto sweep of token budget / batch / chunk size
baselines: vLLM (CUDA), SGLang (CUDA), llama.cpp (Apple Silicon + CUDA); your engine on both devices
metrics: throughput, p99 TTFT/TBT, goodput, peak memory; configs + run cards checked in
output: comparison matrix + a defended target regime for the optimization
papers: Sarathi-Serve (OSDI'24); vLLM/SGLang/llama.cpp as baselines
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the chunking policy, the Pareto sweep, and the comparison methodology. Use production engines as external baselines — same model, traces, metrics. Be honest about where they win and where they don't.
implementation_steps: Implement chunked prefill + stall-free batching; verify oracle parity; sweep the Pareto frontier per device.
Stand up vLLM and SGLang on a rented CUDA GPU and llama.cpp on the M4 (and CUDA) with the same model/quant.
Run the shared traces through every engine on every device; build the comparison matrix from checked-in run cards.
Name the target regime (where you match/beat: Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low concurrency); write the report's benchmark section.
starter_interfaces: bench/baselines/       # vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp launch + config
bench/compare.py       # engine x device x trace matrix
lessons.exit_gate: Chunked prefill matches the oracle; a reproducible matrix reports the engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across CUDA and Apple Silicon; a defended target regime is chosen; the section is written.

further_reading.name: CSE 234: Data Systems for Machine Learning

primary_resource.section: MLSys Book: serving systems + benchmarking methodology

lessons.title: Weeks 9-11 · Outperformance optimization (3 weeks, data-driven)
lessons.problem: With an honest baseline and a named target regime, implement ONE paper-backed optimization deeply and demonstrate a reproducible win over the production baseline there — preserving output quality. This is the differentiator; it gets three weeks.
lessons.why_it_matters: A concrete 'beat the production system in this regime, here is the number, here is why' result is the sentence that gets the interview. Depth on one optimization — not a pile of half-built ones — is the anti-toy signal.
lessons.build_it: Pick the optimization from the menu using the Week-8 baseline: attack the dimension where you lose most in the target regime.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the benchmark harness; check parity against the oracle; record the ablation delta.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests and the measurement, and write the corresponding report section.

exercises.prompt: Pick the optimization from the menu using the Week-8 baseline: attack the dimension where you lose most in the target regime.
exercises.completion_gate: The optimization preserves quality and produces a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; the optimization section is written; optional extensions are noted.

exercises.prompt: Optional extension (only if ahead): a second optimization as another ablation row, or one hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel on a hot path.
exercises.completion_gate: Measured, reproducible, parity-checked, and written into the report.

tests_and_measurements: One paper-backed optimization (chosen data-driven) that reproducibly outperforms the production baseline in the defended regime, quality preserved.
lessons.proof_artifact: One paper-backed optimization (chosen data-driven) that reproducibly outperforms the production baseline in the defended regime, quality preserved.
lessons.objective: Choose one optimization based on the Week-8 baseline (attack the dimension you lose on), implement it on the engine, preserve quality (parity or a measured bounded delta), and re-run the benchmark to show a reproducible regime win.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, the seeded traces, and the running report; production baselines for the benchmark module.
lessons.architecture_config:
target regime (from W7-8): Apple Silicon, memory-constrained (16GB), low-to-moderate concurrency
choose ONE (data-driven from the baseline gap):
  - KV quantization (KIVI 2-bit): ~4x KV in fixed memory -> more concurrency / longer context than baselines [memory-bound]
  - unified-memory KV offload (FlexGen-style on Apple unified memory, no PCIe penalty): larger effective KV [memory-bound, Apple-native]
  - KV eviction / attention sinks (H2O / StreamingLLM): bounded KV for long/streaming [long-context]
  - speculative decoding (EAGLE / self-speculative): single-stream latency win where vLLM spec-decode is unavailable [latency-bound]
  - prefix/radix cache (SGLang RadixAttention): shared-prefix reuse [agent / shared-prompt workloads]
win condition: a reproducible metric beating the production baseline in the target regime, quality preserved
optional stretch: a second optimization as an extra ablation row, or one hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the optimization implementation and the head-to-head measurement in the target regime. Delegate kernels to PyTorch/FlashInfer (one hand-written kernel is an optional stretch). Quality must be preserved.
implementation_steps: Pick the optimization from the menu using the Week-8 baseline: attack the dimension where you lose most in the target regime.
Implement it deeply on the engine; verify quality is preserved (parity or a measured, bounded delta).
Re-run the benchmark in the target regime; show a reproducible win over the production baseline; write the report's optimization section.
Optional extension (only if ahead): a second optimization as another ablation row, or one hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel on a hot path.
starter_interfaces: engine/opt/            # the chosen optimization (kv_quant / offload / evict / spec / radix)
bench/win.py           # head-to-head vs baseline in the target regime
lessons.exit_gate: The optimization preserves quality and produces a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime; the optimization section is written; optional extensions are noted.

primary_resource.section: GPU Mode lectures + the chosen optimization's paper

lessons.title: Week 12 · Paper-like technical report + portfolio release
lessons.problem: Assemble the incrementally-written sections into one paper-like technical report — the deliverable — and ship the portfolio. The report is what a hiring engineer reads in ten minutes and what turns a benchmark into a result.
lessons.why_it_matters: The paper-like report is the single biggest differentiator from a vibe-coded toy: an abstract, a design, an ablation ladder, a regime-specific result, related work, and honest limitations. It signals research-engineering maturity.
lessons.build_it: Assemble the incrementally-written sections; write the abstract, related work, and limitations.
lessons.use_it: Drive it with the seeded traces and the benchmark harness; check parity against the oracle; record the ablation delta.
lessons.ship_it: Publish the module with tests and the measurement, and write the corresponding report section.

exercises.prompt: Assemble the incrementally-written sections; write the abstract, related work, and limitations.
exercises.completion_gate: A paper-like report with an ablation ladder, the cross-engine benchmark, and the regime win — every number reproducible from checked-in configs; the repo and README are published.

exercises.prompt: Publish the repo, README, and the report; link the reproducible benchmark scripts and run cards.
exercises.completion_gate: Measured, reproducible, parity-checked, and written into the report.

tests_and_measurements: A paper-like technical report with a reproducible ablation ladder and the regime-specific outperformance result, plus the published repo and README.
lessons.proof_artifact: A paper-like technical report with a reproducible ablation ladder and the regime-specific outperformance result, plus the published repo and README.
lessons.objective: Assemble and polish the report (abstract, design, evaluation with the per-stage ablation ladder, the regime win, related work, limitations, reproducibility), run a final confirmation sweep, and publish the repo + README.
lessons.exact_inputs: The previous module's artifact, a small model (Qwen2.5-0.5B / Llama-3.2-1B), the frozen oracle, the seeded traces, and the running report; production baselines for the benchmark module.
lessons.architecture_config:
report sections (written incrementally W1-11, assembled now):
  abstract · introduction · engine design (paged KV, batching, chunked prefill, admission)
  evaluation: per-stage ablation ladder + cross-engine/device benchmark + the regime win
  related work (vLLM, SGLang, Orca, Sarathi-Serve, the optimization's paper) · limitations · reproducibility (configs, run cards)
release: repo, README, the report (PDF/Markdown), reproducible benchmark scripts
lessons.abstraction_level: Own the writeup and the reproducibility package. The report separates owned engine code from delegated kernels and baselines, and states the win condition and its scope honestly.
implementation_steps: Assemble the incrementally-written sections; write the abstract, related work, and limitations.
Run a final confirmation sweep so every number in the report is reproducible from checked-in configs.
Build the ablation-ladder figure (each mechanism's contribution) and the regime-win figure.
Publish the repo, README, and the report; link the reproducible benchmark scripts and run cards.
lessons.exit_gate: A paper-like report with an ablation ladder, the cross-engine benchmark, and the regime win — every number reproducible from checked-in configs; the repo and README are published.

projects.title: A from-scratch LLM inference engine: benchmarked per stage, optimized to win a regime, written up as a paper
projects.positioning_statement: MVP-first: build a working engine, then add one mechanism at a time and re-benchmark, so every gain is attributable. Benchmark the complete-but-unoptimized engine against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon; let that baseline pick the optimization; implement it deeply to outperform the production baseline in a defended regime. Deliverable: a paper-like technical report with an ablation ladder.
user_advantage: Reuses Jetson/TensorRT/CUDA experience: constrained memory and latency profiles, paged KV, and measured throughput tradeoffs.
Reuses AWS reliability experience: admission control, backpressure, overload behavior, timeouts, and graceful shutdown.
Apple Silicon advantage: an M4 16GB is the exact regime where vLLM/SGLang are weak or absent, so the outperformance story is credible.
projects.research_question: Built MVP-first and benchmarked per stage against vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp across cloud CUDA and Apple Silicon, which single paper-backed optimization — chosen from the measured baseline gap — lets a from-scratch engine outperform the production baseline in a defended regime, and by how much?
what_you_will_implement: Weeks 1-2: MVP engine + benchmark harness (Mac-first) — A working MVP engine, a frozen oracle, a Mac benchmark harness, and the first MVP-vs-llama.cpp numbers.
Weeks 3-4: Paged KV cache (PagedAttention) + re-benchmark — A PagedAttention KV cache with oracle parity and an attributed memory/concurrency delta over the MVP.
Weeks 5-6: Continuous batching (Orca) + admission + re-benchmark — An Orca continuous-batching scheduler with admission, oracle parity, and an attributed throughput/goodput delta.

Weeks 7-8: Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + full cross-engine benchmark — A Sarathi-Serve chunked-prefill scheduler and a reproducible cross-engine, cross-device benchmark that names the target regime.

Weeks 9-11: Outperformance optimization (3 weeks, data-driven) — One paper-backed optimization (chosen data-driven) that reproducibly outperforms the production baseline in the defended regime, quality preserved.

Week 12: Paper-like technical report + portfolio release — A paper-like technical report with a reproducible ablation ladder and the regime-specific outperformance result, plus the published repo and README.

Optional extension (only if ahead of schedule): a second optimization as another ablation row, a hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel, a prefix/radix cache for agent workloads, or a prefill/decode disaggregation spike.
problem_index: Weeks 7-8 Chunked prefill (Sarathi-Serve) + full cross-engine benchmark
Week 12 Paper-like technical report + portfolio release

implementation_references.title: Outcome School — AI & Machine Learning
implementation_references.use: curriculum reference: LLM internals, vLLM serving, inference-platform system design

owned: the engine: scheduler, paged KV cache, continuous batching, chunked prefill, admission
the per-stage benchmark harness and methodology
delegated: production baselines to vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp
low-level Metal/CUDA kernels (one optional hand-written kernel as a stretch)

proof_gaps: No public from-scratch inference-engine artifact yet.
No measured per-stage ablation vs production systems.
No paper-like technical report demonstrating a regime-specific win.
acceptance_criteria: MVP-first: a working, benchmarked engine exists by Week 2 and at every stage thereafter.
Every mechanism is benchmarked incrementally (vs the previous stage and llama.cpp) — an attributable ablation ladder, not one end-of-project number.
A reproducible cross-engine, cross-device benchmark reports the complete engine vs vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp from checked-in configs.
The optimization is chosen data-driven from the Week-8 baseline and yields a reproducible win over the production baseline in the defended regime, quality preserved.
The deliverable is a paper-like technical report (abstract, design, ablation ladder, regime win, related work, limitations, reproducibility).

resource_decisions.reason: Primary serving-internals reference: vLLM internals, continuous batching, SLOs, and a speculative-decoding server.
Serving-systems vocabulary and benchmarking methodology for the whole engine.

assigned_sections: Ch. 26-32 Concurrency, threads, locks, condition variables — https://pages.cs.wisc.edu/~remzi/OSTEP/
resource_decisions.reason: Concurrency and scheduling primitives for the iteration-level scheduler and admission control.
Block allocation and page-table design, the analog for the paged KV cache.

assigned_sections: Serving systems and the throughput-latency tradeoff — https://hao-ai-lab.github.io/cse234-w25/
resource_decisions.reason: Modern serving systems and the throughput-latency tradeoff.
resource_decisions.resource_name: CSE 234: Data Systems for Machine Learning

assigned_sections: Load, latency percentiles, backpressure, graceful degradation — https://dataintensive.net/
resource_decisions.reason: Reliability under load: percentiles, backpressure, and graceful degradation for admission control.

resource_decisions.trigger: Optional Week 11-12 hand-written CUDA/Metal kernel on a hot path.
resource_decisions.reason: Kernel-level optimization for the optional outperformance-stretch kernel.
Dependency-light, inspectable inference loop as the from-scratch reference.
resource_decisions.resource_name: Karpathy from-scratch LLM repos (nanoGPT, llm.c, llama2.c, nanochat)

cuts.reason: This is an inference-systems project; training a model would displace the engine depth.

One optimization done deeply (with parity, ablations, and a paper-like writeup) beats a pile of half-built ones — feature-stacking is a toy tell. Extra optimizations are optional ablation rows, not core scope.
cuts.revisit_when: Optional extension lane, only if ahead of schedule.
topics: KIVI / H2O / StreamingLLM / EAGLE / RadixAttention as optional extra ablation rows

cuts.item: Beating vLLM/SGLang on datacenter GPUs at scale
cuts.reason: Naive and falsifiable. The defended claim is a regime win: Apple Silicon, memory-constrained, low-to-moderate concurrency.
cuts.revisit_when: Never as framed; the regime-specific claim is the honest one.

cuts.reason: Needs multi-GPU and network KV transfer — out of reach solo. A disaggregation spike is an optional extension.
Own the algorithms; delegate kernels to PyTorch/FlashInfer. One hand-written kernel is an optional extension.

compute_policy.canonical_environment: MacBook Pro M4 16GB is the primary dev + Apple-Silicon benchmark device (MPS/Metal, small model). The full cross-engine benchmark runs on a short-lived rented CUDA GPU (your engine + vLLM + SGLang) at Week 8 and after the optimization. llama.cpp is benchmarked on both the M4 and CUDA. Cloud is used only when there is a mature engine worth comparing.
compute_policy.expected_total_spend: Development is Mac-local and free. Paid compute is the benchmark legs on a rented 24GB CUDA GPU (~USD0.27-0.69/hour); a handful of benchmark hours imply roughly USD15-40. Plan USD20-50 expected, USD150 hard ceiling.
compute_policy.per_run_gate: Cloud runs only after the Mac-local module passes its parity gate; each benchmark run is scripted from checked-in configs and run cards so it is reproducible and short.

execution_timeline.title: 12-week execution timeline: June 22 to September 13, 2026 (MVP-first, ~182h committed)
execution_timeline.capacity_rule: ~17 committed + ~3 reserve h/week; ~182h committed with ~22h slack. The report is written incrementally; Week 12 is assembly. Optional extensions only if ahead of schedule.
parallelization_rules: MVP-first and Mac-first: a working engine by Week 2; all engine logic developed/tested on the M4 (MPS); the same code runs on CUDA.
Benchmark incrementally every stage (vs the previous stage + llama.cpp on Mac); the full cross-engine benchmark vs vLLM/SGLang on rented CUDA runs at Week 8 and again after the optimization.
The optimization is data-driven: pick it from the Week-8 baseline gap, not in advance. Optional extensions are stretch-only.
